title: Torque Converter Nm/lb·ft/kg·m 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Torque Unit Converter: Nm, lb·ft, and kg·m
description: Free torque converter. Instantly convert between Newton-meters, foot-pounds, and kilogram-meters. Fast & accurate calculations.
intro: This torque converter instantly transforms values between the three most common torque units: Newton-meters (Nm), pound-feet (lb·ft), and kilogram-meters (kg·m). Torque measures the rotational force applied around an axis — defined as Force × Moment Arm. Engine torque ratings, lug-nut tightening specs, and industrial fastener tables all rely on precise unit conversions. The key conversion factors are: **1 Nm = 0.737562 lb·ft = 0.101972 kg·m**. Use this tool whenever you're reading a European car manual (Nm) but working with a US torque wrench (lb·ft), or verifying manufacturer specs across different unit systems.
keyTakeaway: **1 Nm = 0.737562 lb·ft = 0.101972 kg·m** — e.g., a typical car engine producing 350 Nm equals 258.1 lb·ft or 35.7 kg·m.
useCases: Converting a European car's peak engine torque spec (e.g., 400 Nm) to lb·ft for use with a US-calibrated torque wrench when re-torquing cylinder head bolts.
Verifying wheel lug-nut tightening specifications: a common spec of 100 lb·ft equals 135.6 Nm — critical to avoid over- or under-torquing and wheel detachment.
Translating Japanese motorcycle service manuals that list torque in kg·m (e.g., 3.5 kg·m for an axle nut) into Nm or lb·ft for use with non-metric tools.
Checking industrial machinery fastener specs published in kg·m against ANSI bolt-torque tables expressed in lb·ft to ensure compliance with equipment installation requirements.
Comparing diesel truck engine torque figures across brands — e.g., Cummins specs in lb·ft vs. Mercedes-Benz in Nm — for fleet purchasing decisions.
Calculating the proper setting on a click-type torque wrench (which reads in lb·ft) to match a brake caliper bolt spec given in Nm in a German OEM service bulletin.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

Torque is defined as the cross product of force and lever arm length: **τ = F × r**. The unit conversions derive directly from the SI definitions of force and length.

```
# Exact NIST-traceable conversion factors

1 Nm  = 0.7375621 lb·ft   (exact: 1 / 1.3558179)
1 Nm  = 0.1019716 kg·m    (exact: 1 / 9.80665)

1 lb·ft = 1.3558179 Nm    (exact, from 1 lbf = 4.4482216 N, 1 ft = 0.3048 m)
1 lb·ft = 0.1382550 kg·m

1 kg·m  = 9.80665 Nm      (exact, from g = 9.80665 m/s²)
1 kg·m  = 7.2330139 lb·ft

# General conversion formulas
Result_lbft = Value_Nm  × 0.7375621
Result_Nm   = Value_lbft × 1.3558179
Result_kgm  = Value_Nm  × 0.1019716
Result_Nm   = Value_kgm  × 9.80665
Result_kgm  = Value_lbft × 0.1382550
Result_lbft = Value_kgm  × 7.2330139
```

> **Note on kg·m:** This unit uses kilogram-force (kgf), not kilogram-mass. The kilogram-force is defined as the force exerted by 1 kg of mass under standard gravity (g = 9.80665 m/s²), per NIST Special Publication 811.

---

## Reference Table

| Nm | lb·ft | kg·m | Common Application |
|---------|--------|--------|---------------------------------------------|
| 10 | 7.38 | 1.020 | Small engine drain plug |
| 25 | 18.44 | 2.549 | Spark plug (most gasoline engines) |
| 47 | 34.67 | 4.793 | Typical lug nut (compact car, metric) |
| 100 | 73.76 | 10.197 | Lug nut (mid-size sedan, metric spec) |
| 135 | 99.62 | 13.77 | Lug nut (100 lb·ft spec, US trucks) |
| 150 | 110.6 | 15.30 | Cylinder head bolt (many 4-cyl engines) |
| 250 | 184.4 | 25.49 | Connecting rod bolt (high-performance) |
| 350 | 258.1 | 35.70 | Mid-size car peak engine torque |
| 550 | 405.7 | 56.08 | V8 muscle car peak engine torque |
| 881 | 650.0 | 89.84 | Dodge RAM 1500 TRX (Hellcat, 650 lb·ft) |
| 1,627 | 1,200 | 166.0 | Heavy-duty diesel truck (e.g., Cummins X15) |

---

## Typical Examples

### Example 1 — European Sedan Engine Spec
A BMW 3 Series turbocharged inline-6 is rated at **400 Nm** of peak torque in the service manual. A US technician needs this in lb·ft:

```
400 Nm × 0.7375621 = 295.0 lb·ft
400 Nm × 0.1019716 = 40.79 kg·m
```
The technician sets the torque wrench to **295 lb·ft**.

### Example 2 — Japanese Motorcycle Axle Nut
A Honda CBR service manual lists the rear axle nut torque as **8.8 kg·m**:

```
8.8 kg·m × 9.80665 = 86.30 Nm
8.8 kg·m × 7.2330  = 63.65 lb·ft
```
Using a lb·ft wrench, the technician torques to **63.65 lb·ft ≈ 64 lb·ft**.

### Example 3 — US Truck Lug Nut
A Ford F-150 specifies lug nuts at **150 lb·ft**:

```
150 lb·ft × 1.3558179 = 203.4 Nm
150 lb·ft × 0.1382550 = 20.74 kg·m
```
A mechanic with only a Nm-calibrated wrench sets it to **203 Nm**.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Confusing lb·ft with lb·in (pound-inches):** Pound-inches are 12× smaller than pound-feet. Torquing a spark plug to "25 lb·ft" when the spec is "25 lb·in" (= 2.08 lb·ft = 2.82 Nm) will strip threads instantly. Always check the unit prefix.

2. **Using mass-kg instead of force-kg (kgf):** The kg·m unit means kilogram-*force*·meter. Treating 1 kg·m as simply 1 kg × 1 m without applying g = 9.80665 m/s² gives a result ~2% off and is physically incorrect per NIST SP 811.

3. **Rounding too early:** Using 0.738 instead of 0.7375621 for Nm→lb·ft introduces a 0.034% error — negligible for most use cases, but on safety-critical bolts (e.g., brake calipers at 250 Nm), this could mean an error of 0.085 lb·ft. Always carry at least 4 significant figures.

4. **Applying torque without accounting for friction/angle:** A torque-to-yield (TTY) bolt spec often combines a torque value (e.g., 60 Nm) *plus* an angle (e.g., +90°). Converting only the Nm value without noting the angle method leads to undertorqued fasteners and potential head gasket failure.

5. **Ignoring temperature effects on torque wrenches:** NIST calibration standards indicate that torque wrenches can drift ±4% when used outside their calibrated temperature range. A wrench calibrated at 70°F used in a 20°F shop may under-torque by up to 4% even with a perfect unit conversion.

---

## Related Calculators

Torque conversions are closely related to other mechanical and engineering calculations. While no dedicated related calculators are currently linked, you may find these topics useful:

- **Horsepower ↔ Torque ↔ RPM**: Power (hp) = Torque (lb·ft) × RPM ÷ 5252
- **Force & Pressure converters**: For computing bolt clamping load from torque specs
- **Unit converters for length and force**: Foundational for understanding torque derivation

faq.q: What is the exact conversion factor between Nm and lb·ft?
faq.a: The exact factor is 1 lb·ft = 1.3558179 Nm, derived from 1 lbf = 4.4482216 N and 1 ft = 0.3048 m, both NIST-defined exact values. Therefore 1 Nm = 0.7375621 lb·ft. This is the factor used in NIST Special Publication 811 and all major engineering standards bodies (SAE, ISO).

faq.q: Why does kg·m use 9.80665 and not just 9.8 or 10?
faq.a: The value 9.80665 m/s² is the internationally defined standard acceleration of gravity (g₀), established by the 3rd General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM) in 1901 and reaffirmed by NIST. Using 9.8 introduces a 0.068% error; using 10 introduces a 1.97% error — significant for precision fastener work. Always use 9.80665 for traceable results.

faq.q: Is lb·ft the same as ft·lb?
faq.a: Yes — lb·ft and ft·lb refer to the same unit: pound-force times feet. The notation 'lb·ft' (force × distance) is technically preferred by SAE and ASME to distinguish it from ft·lb used in energy (foot-pounds of work). In practice, both notations are interchangeable in torque contexts, and all major OEM service manuals treat them identically.

faq.q: What are typical torque specs for common automotive fasteners?
faq.a: Common specs: spark plugs 10–30 Nm (7–22 lb·ft); lug nuts 80–165 Nm (60–122 lb·ft) depending on vehicle; cylinder head bolts 50–200 Nm (37–148 lb·ft); oil drain plugs 20–40 Nm (15–30 lb·ft); brake caliper bolts 25–130 Nm (18–96 lb·ft). Always consult the vehicle-specific service manual — these vary significantly by fastener size (M8–M14) and material.

faq.q: Why do US car specs use lb·ft while European specs use Nm?
faq.a: The US automotive industry historically used imperial units (lb·ft) based on the foot-pound-second (FPS) system. European manufacturers adopted SI (metric) units, standardizing on Newton-meters, following the 1960 international adoption of SI by the General Conference on Weights and Measures. Japanese manufacturers often used kg·m historically but have largely transitioned to Nm in modern service manuals, reflecting global SI adoption.

faq.q: How accurate do I need to be when converting torque for fastener work?
faq.a: For general maintenance (oil changes, body panels), rounding to the nearest whole number in lb·ft or Nm is sufficient. For safety-critical fasteners — lug nuts, brake calipers, suspension components, and cylinder heads — use at least 3 significant figures (e.g., 135.6 Nm, not 136 Nm). Over-torquing by 10–15% can permanently stretch or snap bolts; under-torquing by the same margin risks loosening under vibration.

faq.q: Can I use this converter for industrial machinery and not just automotive use?
faq.a: Absolutely. The conversion factors (1 Nm = 0.7375621 lb·ft = 0.1019716 kg·m) are universal physics constants — they apply equally to industrial gearboxes, electric motor shaft torque ratings (NEMA standards use lb·ft; IEC standards use Nm), robotics (Nm is standard in ROS/ISO 9283), and aerospace fastener specs (MIL-HDBK-60). The math is identical regardless of application.

faq.q: What's the difference between torque and work/energy if both can be measured in Nm?
faq.a: Torque (Nm) and energy/work (Joules, also Nm) are dimensionally identical but physically distinct. Torque is a vector — it describes rotational force at a point, not energy transferred. One Joule of work = force × displacement through space. One Nm of torque = force × lever arm, with no implied motion. The SAE and NIST both recommend using 'N·m' for torque and 'J' for energy to avoid confusion, even though the base units are the same.

sources.name: NIST Special Publication 811 – Guide for the Use of the International System of Units (SI)
NIST – Unit Conversion Factors (Appendix B, SP 811)
